file_name: Cerus Corporation - FIRST AMEND TO SUPPLY AND MANUFACTURING AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: Unless otherwise expressly provided in the applicable Statement of Work payment to Porex of undisputed fees shall be due [*] days following Cerus' receipt of the invoice submitted by Porex.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless otherwise expressly provided in the applicable Statement of Work payment to Porex of undisputed fees shall be due 90 days following Cerus' receipt of the invoice submitted by Porex.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the payment term to 90 days may violate prompt payment laws, especially for small businesses.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Prompt Payment Act - Cal. Pub. Contract Code § 10262.5
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Prompt Payment Act (Cal. Pub. Contract Code § 10262.5) mandates specific payment timelines for state agencies, and while this contract might not directly involve a state agency, excessively long payment terms (90 days) can be deemed commercially unreasonable and could be challenged, particularly if Porex is a smaller supplier dependent on timely payments.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1100 - 22355] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1981, Ch. 306. ) PART 2. CONTRACTING BY STATE AGENCIES [10100 - 19150] ( Heading of Part 2 added by Stats. 1982, Ch. 1120, Sec. 6. ) CHAPTER 1. State Contract Act [10100 - 10285.5] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1981, Ch. 306. ) ARTICLE 8. Modifications; Performance; Payment [10250 - 10265] ( Article 8 enacted by Stats. 1981, Ch. 306. ) 10262.5. (a) Notwithstanding any other law, a prime contractor or subcontractor shall pay to any subcontractor, not later than seven days after receipt of each progress payment, the respective amounts allowed the contractor on account of the work performed by the subcontractors, to the extent of each subcontractor’s interest therein. In the event that there is a good faith dispute over all or any portion of the amount due on a progress payment from the prime contractor or subcontractor to a subcontractor, then the prime contractor or subcontractor may withhold no more than 150 percent of the disputed amount. Any contractor who violates this section shall pay to the subcontractor a penalty of 2 percent of the amount due per month for every month that payment is not made. In any action for the collection of funds wrongfully withheld, the prevaili
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the payment term to 90 days. This contradicts California's Prompt Payment Act, as explained in the 'Law Explanation,' especially if Porex is a smaller supplier. Scraped Law Snippet 1, PCC 10262.5(a), mandates that prime contractors or subcontractors must pay their subcontractors within seven days of receiving each progress payment. While the contract might not fall under the direct purview of PCC 10262.5, extending the payment term to 90 days creates an inconsistency because it vastly exceeds the prompt payment timeframe contemplated by law, making the contract unenforceable to that point.

perturbation.original_text: Cerus shall have the right to accept or reject the Services, or any portion thereof, in writing within [*] days from Porex's completion of the Services. Such acceptance or rejection shall be consistent with the criteria set forth in the Statement of Work, if any. If Cerus does not reject in writing within [*] days, the Services shall be considered accepted by Cerus. Within [*] days of any notice of rejection, Porex shall present a corrective plan of action to Cerus.
perturbation.changed_text: Cerus shall have the right to accept or reject the Services, or any portion thereof, in writing within 60 days from Porex's completion of the Services. Such acceptance or rejection shall be consistent with the criteria set forth in the Statement of Work, if any. If Cerus does not reject in writing within 60 days, the Services shall be considered accepted by Cerus. Within 5 days of any notice of rejection, Porex shall present a corrective plan of action to Cerus.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring a corrective plan of action within only 5 days may be considered an unreasonably short timeframe, potentially violating fair business practice standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly defining a timeframe, UCC § 2-602 implies that rejection of goods (or, by analogy, services) must be within a 'reasonable time.' Mandating a corrective action plan within just 5 days after rejection may not afford Porex a reasonable opportunity to assess and respond, potentially conflicting with the UCC's emphasis on fair dealing and commercial reasonableness.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-602. Manner and Effect of Rightful Rejection. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-602. Manner and Effect of Rightful Rejection. (1) Rejection of goods must be within a reasonable time after their delivery or tender. It is ineffective unless the buyer seasonably notifies the seller . (2) Subject to the provisions of the two following sections on rejected goods (Sections 2-603 and 2-604 ), (a) after rejection any exercise of ownership by the buyer with respect to any commercial unit is wrongful as against the seller ; and (b) if the buyer has before rejection taken physical possession of goods in which he does not have a security interest under the provisions of this Article (subsection (3) of Section 2-711 ), he is under a duty after rejection to hold them with reasonable care at the seller's disposition for a time sufficient to permit the seller to remove them; but (c) the buyer has no further obligations with regard to goods rightfully rejected. (3) The seller's rights with respect to goods wrongfully rejected are governed by the provisions of this Article on seller's remedies in general (Section 2-703 ). Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text potentially contradicts UCC § 2-602 regarding 'reasonable time' for rejection. The original contract had an unspecified timeframe ('[*] days') for Porex to present a corrective plan. The changed text specifies 5 days, which, as the Perturbation and Law Explanations suggest, might not be 'reasonable' according to UCC standards, specifically UCC § 2-602. Manner and Effect of Rightful Rejection. (1) Rejection of goods must be within a reasonable time after their delivery or tender.' This creates a potential legal contradiction by imposing a potentially unreasonable deadline.

perturbation.original_text: Cerus will not have responsibility to pay for identified errors, incomplete or inaccurate items (collectively the "invoice errors") which result in increases to previously invoiced amounts if the aforementioned invoice errors are communicated to Cerus later than [*] days from the original invoice date.
perturbation.changed_text: Cerus will not have responsibility to pay for identified errors, incomplete or inaccurate items (collectively the "invoice errors") which result in increases to previously invoiced amounts if the aforementioned invoice errors are communicated to Cerus later than 10 days from the original invoice date.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the timeframe to identify invoice errors to just 10 days from the invoice date is unreasonably short and could violate general contract law principles of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. A 10-day window to identify and report invoice errors may be deemed commercially unreasonable, especially for complex invoices, potentially violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text, by limiting the timeframe to identify invoice errors to 10 days, contradicts the obligation of good faith as outlined in UCC § 1-304. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight that this short timeframe may be deemed commercially unreasonable, especially for complex invoices. Scraped Law Snippet 1 directly states that 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By imposing an unreasonably short deadline, the modified contract could be interpreted as acting in bad faith, creating a direct contradiction with the UCC.
